title: Food Protein-Induced Enterocolitis Syndrome (FPIES)
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Allergy/Immunology Fellows, Pediatrics, Emergency Medicine, Gastroenterology
objectives: Understand FPIES as a non-IgE mediated food allergy
Differentiate acute and chronic FPIES phenotypes
Recognize common trigger foods by age and geography
Apply diagnostic criteria from the 2017 International Consensus Guidelines
Manage acute FPIES reactions including severe presentations
Understand natural history and approach to oral food challenges

guidelineReferences.name: International Consensus Guidelines for FPIES 2017
keyPoints: Diagnostic criteria based on clinical history
OFC is gold standard for diagnosis

vignette.text:
A 6-month-old boy is brought to the ED by his parents. Approximately 2.5 hours after eating rice cereal for the first time, he developed repetitive, projectile vomiting (5 episodes over 1 hour). He became pale, lethargic, and floppy.

He is exclusively breastfed and this was his first introduction of solid food. No prior similar episodes.

Past Medical History: Full-term, healthy, mild eczema

Family History: Mother with seasonal allergies

Medications: None

Allergies: None known

physicalExam.general: Lethargic, pale, limp infant, irritable when aroused
physicalExam.HEENT: Sunken fontanelle, dry mucous membranes, no oral lesions
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, weak pulses, capillary refill 4 seconds
physicalExam.pulmonary: Clear, no wheezing or respiratory distress
physicalExam.abdomen: Mildly distended, no masses, decreased bowel sounds
physicalExam.skin: Pallor, no urticaria, no angioedema, no rash, mild xerosis on cheeks

diagnostics.stool: Negative for blood, negative for infection

questions.stem: This infant has severe GI symptoms hours after eating a new food.
questions.question: What is FPIES and how is it classified?

options.text: FPIES = Food Protein-Induced Enterocolitis Syndrome; NON-IgE mediated (cell-mediated) food allergy; Primarily affects GI tract (enterocolitis); THREE PHENOTYPES: ACUTE: Intermittent ingestion, symptoms 1-4h after ingestion (vomiting), can be severe with shock; CHRONIC: Daily ingestion, ongoing symptoms (vomiting, diarrhea, FTT); ATYPICAL: Positive specific IgE to trigger (may progress to IgE-mediated allergy)
Always involves skin and respiratory symptoms

questions.explanation: FPIES (Food Protein-Induced Enterocolitis Syndrome): KEY FEATURES: Non-IgE mediated, cell-mediated food allergy. Part of spectrum including food protein-induced proctocolitis and enteropathy. Primarily affects GI tract (inflammation of small and large intestine). No urticaria, angioedema, or respiratory symptoms (distinguishes from IgE-mediated). THREE PHENOTYPES: ACUTE FPIES: Intermittent exposure to trigger. Symptoms 1-4 hours after ingestion. Profuse vomiting (hallmark), later diarrhea. Can progress to lethargy, pallor, hypotension, hypothermia, shock (~20%). CHRONIC FPIES: Daily/regular ingestion of trigger. Intermittent vomiting, chronic diarrhea, poor weight gain/FTT. Resolves when trigger eliminated. ATYPICAL FPIES: Has positive specific IgE to trigger food. May be transitioning to IgE-mediated allergy. Requires epinephrine in addition to FPIES management.

questions.stem: You want to understand why this infant became so ill.
questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of FPIES?

options.text: POORLY UNDERSTOOD, but cell-mediated: T-cell mediated response to food protein; Increased intestinal permeability; Inflammatory cytokine release (TNF-α, others); Leads to: Fluid shifts into gut lumen (third-spacing), Intestinal inflammation, Systemic inflammatory response; NOT IgE-mediated: Skin prick tests and specific IgE typically NEGATIVE; Epinephrine NOT effective for typical FPIES (no IgE involvement)

questions.explanation: FPIES pathophysiology (incompletely understood): CELL-MEDIATED MECHANISM: T-cell activation to food proteins. Increased gut permeability. Release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α prominent). Local intestinal inflammation. CLINICAL CONSEQUENCES: Fluid and protein loss into GI lumen (third-spacing) → dehydration, hypovolemia. Systemic inflammatory response → metabolic acidosis, methemoglobinemia (seen in this patient). Vomiting from intestinal inflammation/ileus. KEY DISTINCTION FROM IgE ALLERGY: NOT IgE-mediated. Skin prick tests: Usually NEGATIVE. Specific IgE: Usually NEGATIVE (this patient had negative rice IgE). Epinephrine: NOT effective (no mast cell degranulation). Antihistamines: NOT effective. This is why FPIES is often missed and misdiagnosed as sepsis or viral gastroenteritis.

questions.stem: Rice cereal was the trigger in this infant.
questions.question: What are the common FPIES trigger foods?

options.text: INFANTS/CHILDREN - varies by geography: US/Australia: Cow's milk, soy, rice (most common), oats; Europe: Fish more common; Japan: Egg, wheat; Grains often trigger: Rice, oats, barley (can be hypoallergenic for IgE but cause FPIES!); ANY food can cause FPIES, even 'hypoallergenic' foods; ADULTS: Shellfish, fish, mollusks most common; Often onset in adulthood; More severe reactions reported
Peanut is the most common trigger

questions.explanation: FPIES trigger foods: INFANTS (most common presentation): Cow's milk: Most common overall, especially formula-fed. Soy: Common, often reacts with milk. Grains: RICE (very common in US/Australia - this patient!), Oats, Barley. Important: These are often recommended as 'hypoallergenic' first foods for IgE allergy but can trigger FPIES! Other: Fish, egg, poultry, vegetables (varies by region). Geographic variation: Japan - egg, wheat more common; Europe - fish more common. IMPORTANT: ANY food can cause FPIES, including avocado, sweet potato, banana. ADULTS: Shellfish and mollusks most common (crustaceans, fish). Often new-onset in adulthood. May have more severe reactions. BREASTFEEDING: Very rare to react to proteins in breastmilk. FPIES typically begins with direct food introduction. This patient: Rice cereal - classic solid-food FPIES trigger at typical age of introduction.

questions.stem: You need to confirm the diagnosis of FPIES.
questions.question: What are the diagnostic criteria for acute FPIES?

options.text: 2017 Consensus MAJOR criterion: Vomiting 1-4 hours after ingestion of suspect food, absence of classic IgE-mediated symptoms; MINOR criteria (≥3 for diagnosis): Lethargy; Pallor; Need for ED visit; Need for IV fluid support; Diarrhea within 24 hours; Hypotension; Hypothermia; Second episode with SAME food is diagnostic; Lab findings supportive: Neutrophilia, thrombocytosis, methemoglobinemia, acidosis (all non-specific)

questions.explanation: Diagnostic criteria (2017 International Consensus): ACUTE FPIES DIAGNOSIS requires: MAJOR CRITERION: Vomiting in the 1-4 hour period after ingestion of suspected food, AND Absence of classic IgE-mediated allergic skin or respiratory symptoms. PLUS ≥3 MINOR CRITERIA: (1) A second (or more) episode of repetitive vomiting after eating same food, (2) Repetitive vomiting 1-4h after different food, (3) Extreme lethargy, (4) Marked pallor, (5) Need for ED visit, (6) Need for IV fluid support, (7) Diarrhea within 24h, (8) Hypotension, (9) Hypothermia. LAB FINDINGS (supportive, not diagnostic): Leukocytosis with neutrophil left shift (peaks ~6h), Thrombocytosis, Methemoglobinemia (seen in this patient!), Metabolic acidosis, Stool may have blood, mucus, leukocytes. This patient: Vomiting 2.5h after rice + lethargy + pallor + hypotension + hypothermia + tachycardia + need for ED/IVF = meets criteria.

questions.question: What is the differential diagnosis for FPIES?

options.text: Common misdiagnoses: SEPSIS/BACTERIAL INFECTION: Most common misdiagnosis; Similar: lethargy, hypotension, neutrophilia; Different: No fever in FPIES, blood cultures negative; VIRAL GASTROENTERITIS: Similar: vomiting, diarrhea; Different: No temporal food relationship, often fever; NECROTIZING ENTEROCOLITIS (neonates): Consider in premature infants; IgE-MEDIATED FOOD ALLERGY: Different: Has urticaria/angioedema/wheeze, occurs within minutes, skin test positive

questions.explanation: Differential diagnosis for FPIES: SEPSIS/BACTERIAL INFECTION: Most common misdiagnosis! Similarities: Lethargy, pallor, hypotension, tachycardia, neutrophilia. Differences: No fever in FPIES (often hypothermic), Blood/stool cultures negative, Onset linked to food ingestion, Resolves with supportive care. ACUTE VIRAL GASTROENTERITIS: Similarities: Vomiting, diarrhea. Differences: No temporal relationship to specific food, Often has fever, Others may be sick. NECROTIZING ENTEROCOLITIS: Consider in neonates, especially premature. Similar abdominal distension. Imaging and clinical course differ. IgE-MEDIATED FOOD ALLERGY (anaphylaxis): Key differences: Immediate onset (minutes, not hours), Urticaria, angioedema, wheezing present, Positive skin prick test/specific IgE, Responds to epinephrine. INTUSSUSCEPTION: Consider with colicky pain. This patient: No fever (hypothermic), clear temporal food relationship, no skin/respiratory symptoms → FPIES.

questions.stem: This infant is hypotensive and lethargic.
questions.question: How is acute FPIES managed?

options.text: SUPPORTIVE CARE is mainstay: (1) IV FLUID RESUSCITATION: NS or LR bolus 20 mL/kg, repeat as needed for hypovolemia/shock; (2) ONDANSETRON: IV 0.15 mg/kg (max 16 mg), can stop vomiting and shorten reaction; (3) MONITORING: Vital signs, hydration status, labs if severe; EPINEPHRINE: NOT indicated for typical FPIES (not IgE-mediated); Exception: Give if ATYPICAL FPIES (positive IgE) or if anaphylaxis cannot be excluded; Most reactions resolve in hours with supportive care
Epinephrine is first-line for all FPIES

questions.explanation: Acute FPIES management: SUPPORTIVE CARE IS PRIMARY: (1) IV FLUID RESUSCITATION: Normal saline or lactated Ringer's, 20 mL/kg bolus, repeat as needed. Goal: Restore intravascular volume, correct dehydration. Critical in severe reactions with hypotension. (2) ONDANSETRON: IV 0.15 mg/kg (max 16 mg single dose). Can terminate vomiting and shorten reaction duration. Consider for moderate-severe reactions. (3) MONITORING: Continuous vitals in severe reactions, Labs: CBC, metabolic panel, Observation until stable. MEDICATIONS NOT INDICATED: EPINEPHRINE: Not effective for typical FPIES (no IgE involvement). ONLY give if: Atypical FPIES (positive IgE), Cannot exclude anaphylaxis. ANTIHISTAMINES: Not effective. STEROIDS: Limited evidence, generally not used acutely. PROGNOSIS: Most reactions resolve within hours of removing trigger and providing fluids. This patient: Needs IV bolus, ondansetron, monitoring. Will likely improve rapidly.

questions.stem: You need to counsel the family about ongoing management.
questions.question: What is the long-term management of FPIES?

options.text: STRICT AVOIDANCE of trigger food(s); DIETARY GUIDANCE: If milk FPIES → extensively hydrolyzed or amino acid formula; If solid-food FPIES → introduce other foods carefully; Low-risk foods: fruits, vegetables (except in individual triggers); Higher-risk foods: grains (rice, oats), fish, egg; FOOD INTRODUCTION: Consider supervised introduction of new foods in high-risk patients; OFC (Oral Food Challenge) periodically to assess resolution; EMERGENCY PLAN: Letter for school/ED explaining FPIES and management
Carry epinephrine auto-injector for all FPIES

questions.explanation: Long-term FPIES management: STRICT AVOIDANCE: Complete elimination of confirmed trigger food(s). No amount is 'safe' - even small exposures can trigger reactions. FORMULA RECOMMENDATIONS (for infant milk/soy FPIES): First-line: Extensively hydrolyzed casein formula (e.g., Alimentum, Nutramigen). If reactions continue: Amino acid-based formula (e.g., EleCare, Neocate). Soy formula: NOT recommended if milk FPIES (high cross-reactivity ~30-50%). SOLID FOOD INTRODUCTION: Low-risk first: Fruits and vegetables (pear, banana, sweet potato). Higher-risk (introduce cautiously): Grains (rice, oats), fish, egg, poultry. Consider supervised first feedings for high-risk foods. ORAL FOOD CHALLENGES: Periodic OFC (every 12-18 months) to assess for resolution. Performed under medical supervision with IV access. EMERGENCY PLAN: Written action plan for caregivers, school, ED. Letter explaining FPIES (often unknown to ED staff).

questions.question: What is the natural history of FPIES?

options.text: GENERALLY FAVORABLE prognosis: RESOLUTION: Most children outgrow FPIES by age 3-5 years; Milk/soy: Often resolve by age 3; Grains (rice, oats): May take longer (3-5 years); Fish/shellfish: May persist longer; Some persist into adolescence/adulthood; ATYPICAL FPIES: If specific IgE develops, may progress to IgE-mediated allergy; Requires different management (epinephrine needed); OFC should be performed periodically to assess tolerance
FPIES is lifelong and never resolves

questions.explanation: Natural history of FPIES: RESOLUTION RATES (generally favorable): Milk FPIES: ~50-90% resolve by age 3 years. Soy FPIES: Similar to milk, often resolves by age 2-3. Grain FPIES (rice, oats): May take longer - age 3-5 years. Fish/Shellfish: Often persists longer, especially adult-onset. Overall: Most children outgrow FPIES by school age. FACTORS AFFECTING RESOLUTION: Earlier age of onset → better prognosis. Solid-food FPIES may resolve slower than milk/soy. Atypical FPIES (with positive IgE) may transition to IgE-mediated allergy. ADULT-ONSET FPIES: Increasingly recognized. Seafood most common trigger. May not resolve spontaneously. MONITORING FOR RESOLUTION: Periodic oral food challenges (every 12-18 months). Performed under medical supervision. Check specific IgE before challenge (if positive → different management). This patient: Rice FPIES diagnosed at 6 months - likely to resolve by age 3-5 with avoidance.

questions.stem: This infant has acute FPIES to rice.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: ACUTE MANAGEMENT: (1) IV fluid bolus 20 mL/kg NS, repeat as needed for hypotension; (2) Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg IV; (3) Monitor until stable; (4) NO epinephrine needed (IgE negative, no anaphylaxis features); DIAGNOSIS confirmed: Vomiting 2.5h after rice + lethargy + pallor + hypotension + hypothermia = meets criteria; LONG-TERM: Strict rice avoidance, consider supervised introduction of other grains, hypoallergenic first foods (fruits/vegetables), OFC in 12-18 months; EDUCATION: Written plan, ED letter, anticipatory guidance

questions.explanation: Complete management for this patient: ACUTE (ED): (1) IV ACCESS and FLUID RESUSCITATION: NS 20 mL/kg bolus, repeat for hypotension. (2) ONDANSETRON 0.15 mg/kg IV (max 16 mg) to control vomiting. (3) Monitor: Vitals, hydration status until improved. (4) Labs confirmed: Neutrophilia, methemoglobinemia, acidosis - consistent with FPIES. (5) NO epinephrine (IgE negative, no skin/respiratory symptoms). DIAGNOSIS: Meets 2017 consensus criteria: Major: Vomiting 2.5h after rice, no IgE symptoms; Minor: Lethargy, pallor, hypotension, hypothermia, ED visit, IV fluids needed. DISCHARGE PLAN: Strict rice avoidance. Continue breastfeeding. Introduce other solids cautiously - start with low-risk (fruits, vegetables). Avoid other grains initially (oats, barley) - introduce under supervision. FOLLOW-UP: Allergy referral. Written emergency action plan. Consider OFC for rice in 12-18 months. Monitor for resolution (expected by age 3-5).
